authority_boundary: Hand-authored reader enrichment for the public doctrine cards (deep reading, formal statement, worked example, counterexample, enforcement routes, does-not-prove bound). This file is reader content, NOT support evidence: the axiom support-cover validator does not read it, examples here never compute support, and a generated page may expose this content but never raise the authority of the source it explains. Source of record for the formal_clause itself remains core/axiom_organ_routing.json (axioms), PRINCIPLES.md (principles), ANTI_PRINCIPLES.md (anti-principles); the latex here is a reviewed reader rendering of that clause, not a competing source.
latex_grammar_note: latex fields render at build time through a bounded LaTeX->HTML engine (no CDN, no client JS). Use the supported macro set; write function and operator names with \mathrm{...} or \mathsf{...}; avoid environments, \not, and underscores inside \mathrm{...}. Anything outside the grammar degrades to the raw source as code with a recorded render gap.

records.one_line: A claim is admissible only with a basis, a certificate, and a checker that recomputes and accepts it; a bare assertion is bottom evidence.
records.deep: Microcosm starts from the position that an output is worth nothing until it can be reconstructed. A claim over some basis is admissible only if it arrives with a derivation certificate and a checker that recomputes the verdict and accepts it, and the strength the claim may carry is capped at the strength of that check. A confident sentence, a repeated fixture label, or a model's own answer sits at the bottom of the evidence order until something independent rederives it. This is the floor the rest of the doctrine stands on: it is what lets the system treat "a model said so" and "a record is labelled passed" as zero evidence rather than weak evidence.

formal.latex: \mathrm{adm}(\varphi, B) \iff \exists\, c, K.\ K(B, c, \varphi) = \mathsf{accept} \;\wedge\; \mathrm{strength}(\varphi) \sqsubseteq \mathrm{strength}(K(B, c, \varphi))
formal.reads: A claim over a basis is admissible exactly when some certificate and checker accept it, and the claim's strength is bounded above by the strength of that check.

symbols.meaning: the claim is admissible over its basis
a claim
the basis it is computed from
a derivation certificate
a checker that recomputes the verdict
the position a claim or a check holds in the evidence order, with bare assertion at the bottom
no stronger than

records.governs: When an output is allowed to count as true at all.
records.requires: A basis, a derivation certificate, and a checker that recomputes the verdict and accepts it.
records.refuses: An asserted label treated as truth.

example.text: A replay reads a fixture that declares a monitor caught scheming, recomputes the verdict from the underlying trace, and rejects the run when the declared verdict does not derive from the lower-level evidence.

counterexample.text: A page prints "passed" because a result record's title says so, with nothing recomputing the verdict from the evidence beneath it.

records.does_not_prove: One checker catching one class of label echo does not prove a claim true in general. It bounds the claim; it does not bless it.

reader_ladder.plain: A result counts as true only when the inputs it came from and an independent check can reproduce it. A confident statement on its own is worth nothing.

analogy.text: A sports scoreboard is not trusted just because it shows a number. The game log has to list the plays that add up to that score.

maps.doctrine: the claim
maps.analogy: the number on the scoreboard

maps.doctrine: the checker that recomputes it
maps.analogy: adding up the plays in the game log

analogy.boundary: The game log shows that a score can be recomputed; it does not make a score true in every possible game, and one matching log is not proof the whole season was scored correctly.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It lets the system treat 'a model said so' or 'a record is labelled passed' as zero evidence until something independent recreates the answer.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a checked result is therefore certain. The check bounds how much the result can claim; it does not bless it as true everywhere.

records.one_line: Concentrate trust in one small checker over certificates; the producer that emits a certificate is never the thing that decides it.
records.deep: AX-2 says where the trust lives, not just that a check happened. The thing allowed to decide a claim is a small, separately maintained checker K that reads a certificate; the producer that emitted the certificate, however large, fast, or capable, is outside the trusted base and gets no vote on its own output. This is the architecture that makes AX-1's floor cheap to believe: shrinking the decider to a small K means the part you have to audit stays small even as the producers around it grow, so a clever or broken producer cannot quietly widen what counts as accepted. The bite is that confidence does not transfer from producer to verdict; an answer is trusted to the extent a small checker rederived it, and the strength of any claim is capped by the scope of that checker rather than the reputation of whatever generated the certificate.

formal.reads: A claim is trusted only when some certificate is accepted by a checker K that is separate from whatever produced the certificate and is small enough to audit.

symbols.meaning: the claim is trusted
a claim seeking trust
a certificate a producer emits
the small checker that decides
the component that generated c, kept outside the trusted base
the checker is bounded small enough to audit

records.governs: Where trust is concentrated when a claim is decided: in the small checker, not the producer.
records.requires: A small checker that decides over a certificate, kept separate from the producer that emitted it.
records.refuses: Producer trust replacing checker trust.

example.text: The certificate kernel lab keeps the part that emits certificates separate from a small checker that decides them, and a companion Lean witness compiles a tiny example through a real verifier whose kernel rejects an invalid proof body rather than trusting that the producer meant well.

counterexample.text: A generator's own confidence is taken as the verdict, so a large producer effectively certifies itself and the small checking step is skipped because the output looked authoritative.

records.does_not_prove: A small checker accepting one certificate proves only that this certificate passed this checker; it does not establish the producer is correct in general, that the checker covers every claim the producer can make, or that the kernel is itself bug-free. The scope of the check is the ceiling, and this reader illustration carries no support weight on its own.

reader_ladder.plain: A claim counts as trusted only when a small, separate checker re-derives it from the supporting paper. The thing that produced the answer never gets to approve its own answer, no matter how capable it is. A claim is only as strong as what that small checker actually covered.

analogy.text: At a secure building, the person who printed a visitor pass does not get to wave themselves in; a small front desk checks the pass against its own list and decides entry, and only what the desk actually checks counts.

maps.doctrine: the small separate checker K that decides
maps.analogy: the front desk that checks the pass and decides entry

maps.doctrine: the producer that emitted the certificate, kept outside the trusted base
maps.analogy: the person who printed the pass, who cannot approve their own entry

maps.doctrine: the certificate the checker reads
maps.analogy: the pass presented at the desk

maps.doctrine: claim strength is capped by the checker's scope, not the producer's reputation
maps.analogy: you get in only as far as what the desk actually verifies, not how official the pass looks

analogy.boundary: The desk shows that the pass-printer cannot self-admit and that trust sits with the checker; it does not establish that the desk's list is complete, that every visitor was checked correctly, or that one admitted visitor means the whole building's access is sound.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It keeps the part you have to audit small and fixed even as the systems generating answers grow larger and faster, so a clever or broken producer cannot quietly widen what gets accepted.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a powerful, confident producer can be trusted to vouch for its own output, so the small separate checking step is optional once the output looks authoritative.

records.one_line: Authority over an effect is derived at the moment of acting from dereferenced tokens, policy, proof, and world-state; holding a standing credential is not itself authority.
records.deep: Microcosm refuses to treat a credential as the thing that grants power. Authority for a subject to cause an effect is computed at the point of action from the tokens actually dereferenced, the governing policy, the proof references, and the current world-state, so that ambient identity, a long-lived key, or simply having a tool reachable confers nothing on its own. This is the claim and evidence model applied to action rather than to assertion: just as a label is bottom evidence until something recomputes it, a credential is bottom authority until a derivation over policy and proof admits the specific effect. It bites hardest at the confused-deputy boundary, where a component holds broad standing power and is steered into exercising it on someone else's behalf; here the bound on what may happen has to be derived per effect from declared scope and approval, not inherited from whatever the holder could in principle do.

formal.reads: A subject is authorized for an effect exactly when the authorizer's derivation over the dereferenced tokens, governing policy, proof references, and world-state grants it; merely holding a standing credential never on its own implies a grant.

symbols.meaning: subject u is authorized to cause effect e
the subject requesting an effect
the effect to be authorized
the authorizer's derivation over its inputs
the tokens actually dereferenced, not merely presented
the governing policy
the proof references the authorizer derives over
the current world-state
the authorizer's derived decision to permit the effect
the subject merely possesses a standing credential

records.governs: Whether a subject may cause a given effect, decided per effect at the point of action.
records.requires: A derivation over dereferenced tokens, policy, proof references, and world-state, plus declared scope, approval, and side-effect records bounding any tool effect.
records.refuses: A standing credential treated as authority.

example.text: The governed-mutation authorizer dereferences proof, policy, and rollback evidence for each requested change and refuses the mutation when authorization does not derive from that evidence, so a standing credential alone cannot drive the effect.

counterexample.text: A tool call is allowed to write because the caller holds a broad token and the tool is reachable, with no derivation bounding the effect to a declared scope and approval.

records.does_not_prove: Two authorizers deriving authority for their own effect classes does not prove that every effect in the system is so governed, and these witnesses are inputs to a separately computed support verdict, never support themselves. This reading illustrates the rule and establishes no authority the source does not already carry.

reader_ladder.plain: Holding a key or a token is not permission. Permission is worked out for this specific action, at the moment you act, from the rules and the current situation.

analogy.text: A hotel key card opens one room for the nights you paid for. Holding a card does not mean every door should open for you.

maps.doctrine: the standing credential
maps.analogy: holding the key card

maps.doctrine: authority derived per effect from policy and world-state
maps.analogy: the lock checking this door, tonight, against the booking

analogy.boundary: The card-and-lock picture compresses tokens, policy, proof, and the live world-state into one swipe; real authorization weighs all of those, and the analogy does not show that detail.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It closes the confused-deputy hole, where a component with broad standing power is talked into using it on someone else's behalf.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a valid credential is enough. The credential is only one input; permission still has to be derived for the exact action.

records.one_line: A derived artifact's identity is the digest of its declared content basis; equal basis may be reused, basis drift forces recompute, and a missing basis is bottom.
records.deep: AX-4 fixes what a cached or reused result is allowed to mean. A derived artifact is a function of a declared content basis, and its identity is the digest of that basis, so two results are the same only when the inputs they were computed from are the same, not when they happen to share a name or a path. Equal basis licenses reuse or coalescing; any drift in the basis forces a recompute or a refusal; and a basis that cannot be resolved is bottom rather than an optimistic hit. This is the same admissibility floor as AX-1 pushed down to the build and cache layer: reusing an output because its name matches, while the inputs underneath have changed, is the label-echo failure dressed as a performance optimization, and it is what lets a stale projection or a poisoned cache pass for fresh.

formal.latex: a = f(B) \;\wedge\; \mathrm{id}(a) = H(B) \;\wedge\; \big(\mathrm{reuse}(a) \Rightarrow H(B') = H(B)\big) \;\wedge\; \big(H(B') \neq H(B) \Rightarrow \mathrm{recompute}\big) \;\wedge\; \big(B = \emptyset \Rightarrow \bot\big)
formal.reads: A derived artifact is a function of its basis and is identified by the digest of that basis; reuse is allowed only when the current basis digest equals the stored one, a changed digest forces a recompute, and a missing basis is bottom.

symbols.meaning: a derived artifact
the deterministic derivation from basis to artifact
its declared content basis (the inputs it is computed from)
the artifact's identity
the digest of the basis that serves as that identity
serving the stored artifact instead of recomputing
the current basis at reuse time
rederiving the artifact rather than reusing it
bottom: a missing basis is treated as no result, not a hit

records.governs: When a cached or reused result is allowed to stand in for a fresh derivation.
records.requires: A declared content basis, an identity that is the digest of that basis, and a digest comparison that gates reuse against recompute.
records.refuses: Caching by name across drift.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings non-secret material in by manifest and digest, and refuses to reuse an imported copy when the source digest no longer matches, so a changed input cannot pass as an unchanged result.

counterexample.text: A pipeline serves a cached output because the requested name matches a prior run, while the inputs that produced it have since changed underneath the key.

records.does_not_prove: Refusing a digest mismatch at the named import and work-landing surfaces does not prove that every reuse path in the system is basis-keyed. One required part of this axiom, a stable freshness-basis mismatch code for work landing, is recorded as open unfinished coverage rather than claimed, and admission of this axiom as a root law is independent of whether it is witnessed, strong, or complete.

reader_ladder.plain: A reused or cached result is the same as before only when the exact inputs it was built from are the same, not when it merely shares a name or location. If the inputs changed, it must be rebuilt; if the inputs can't be found, there is no result at all rather than an optimistic hit.

analogy.text: A pharmacy refills a prescription only when it matches the exact current doctor's order, not just because your name is on file; if the order changed it must be re-issued, and if no order can be found you get nothing rather than last time's pills.

maps.doctrine: the derived artifact identified by the digest of its declared basis
maps.analogy: the medication identified by the exact current order it was filled from

maps.doctrine: equal basis licenses reuse
maps.analogy: a matching, unchanged order lets the same refill be dispensed

maps.doctrine: basis drift forces recompute
maps.analogy: a changed order forces a re-issued prescription, not the old one

maps.doctrine: a missing basis is bottom, not a hit
maps.analogy: no findable order means you get nothing, not a guess from history

maps.doctrine: matching name or path is not matching basis
maps.analogy: your name being on file is not the same as a matching current order

analogy.boundary: The pharmacy shows that identity must come from the exact inputs and not the name on file; it does not establish that every counter in the system checks orders this way, and it leaves unaddressed the open coverage gap where some reuse paths are not yet keyed to their inputs.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops a stale or tampered result from passing as fresh just because a name or path matched, which is how a poisoned cache or an out-of-date view sneaks through dressed as a performance win.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a matching name or filename is enough to safely reuse a stored result, so re-checking the underlying inputs is just wasted work.

records.one_line: A composite is only as trusted as its weakest part, missing evidence defaults to blocked rather than passing, and a later stage may lower authority but never raise it without a fresh derivation.
records.deep: AX-5 fixes the default and the direction of trust so that absence and silence cannot be read as permission. The status of a composite is the meet of its parts, so one blocked or unknown component pulls the whole down and no amount of green elsewhere lifts it back up; a missing witness defaults to blocked, not to a silent pass. The monotone half forbids the quiet upgrade: a downstream stage may demote authority freely, but raising it requires a new derivation under AX-1, never a stage that simply asserts a higher status than the evidence it received. This is the axiom that makes the gap the safe state, which is why a release step that finds private-adjacent content or overclaiming language stops rather than shipping, and why an uncovered obligation reads as blocked instead of as quietly satisfied.

formal.reads: A composite's status is the meet of its parts' statuses; a part with no evidence is blocked; and a later status may exceed the earlier one only if some new derivation establishes it.

symbols.meaning: the trust status of a composite c
the meet (greatest lower bound) taken over the parts
the parts the composite is built from
a part of the composite
the evidence available for part p
bottom: blocked, the default when a part's evidence is absent
a later status proposed for the same composite
strictly higher in the trust order than
a fresh derivation
derivation d establishes the higher status

records.governs: The default status of missing evidence and the direction trust is allowed to move across stages.
records.requires: Taking the meet of part statuses, defaulting an unwitnessed part to blocked, and a fresh derivation before any stage raises authority.
records.refuses: A silent default pass.

example.text: The release wording gate runs over the system's own public copy and stops the export when it finds overclaiming language, so a later packaging stage cannot raise the claim above what the evidence beneath it supports.

counterexample.text: A status board marks a composite green because no part reported a failure, treating the absence of a recorded check as if it were a passing one.

records.does_not_prove: Defaulting to blocked and refusing silent upgrades does not prove any composite actually correct or complete; it fixes the safe direction of trust, and an obligation with no admissible witness stays blocked rather than becoming true by default.

reader_ladder.plain: Something built from parts is only as trusted as its weakest required part. A part with no evidence counts as blocked, not as fine, and a later step can lower trust but never quietly raise it.

analogy.text: A plane does not take off because most of the pre-flight checklist passed. One required check left blank keeps it at the gate.

maps.doctrine: the composite is only as trusted as its weakest part
maps.analogy: the flight is grounded by its one failing check

maps.doctrine: missing evidence defaults to blocked
maps.analogy: a blank checklist item counts as not-done, not as done

analogy.boundary: The checklist shows fail-closed status and the weakest-link rule; it does not show that trust here is measured along several separate dimensions at once, not a single pass or fail.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It makes a gap the safe state, so a release that finds private-adjacent content or overclaiming language stops instead of shipping.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That enough green parts can outvote a blocked one. They cannot; the weakest required part sets the ceiling.

records.one_line: Not proving a claim is not proving its negation; an absence becomes a negative only inside a declared finite domain whose coverage is complete, and otherwise stays unknown.
records.deep: Microcosm refuses the cheap move of reading a blank as a no. Failing to prove a claim does not license proving its negation; the most an unproven claim earns is unknown, and unknown is a distinct verdict from refuted. The one case where absence does become a negative is narrow: the domain has to be explicitly declared finite and its coverage has to be complete, so that "nothing found" is backed by "everything was looked at." This is what keeps the system from converting gaps in its own map into false confidence: a missing relation, an unmapped region, or a fact with no cited evidence reads as open and computable, not as settled, and the limit is recorded rather than papered over.

formal.reads: If a claim is unproven, its negation may be concluded only when the domain is a declared finite one and coverage over that domain is complete; in every other case the verdict is unknown, never refuted.

symbols.meaning: a claim under consideration
the claim has not been proven
the domain D is explicitly declared finite (closed-world)
coverage over D is complete, so nothing in it was left unexamined
the open-world verdict, distinct from refuted

records.governs: Whether an absence of evidence is allowed to count as a negative result, and inside what scope.
records.requires: An explicitly declared finite domain with complete coverage before any closed-world conclusion; outside it, an unproven claim yields unknown rather than its negation.
records.refuses: Absence of evidence treated as evidence of absence.

example.text: The self-ignorance coverage ledger tracks the gap between what has been declared in scope and what has actually been materialized, and forbids the inference that an unmapped region is empty; the open space stays a recorded, computable gap rather than a quiet claim of completeness.

counterexample.text: A status surface reports a property as clean because a scan returned no findings, without ever declaring the finite domain the scan covered, so silence over an unbounded space is read as a guarantee.

records.does_not_prove: Tracking a declared-versus-materialized gap does not exhaust the space of unknown unknowns or prove the unmapped region empty. The open-world unknown it preserves is a coverage state, not a support verdict, and it never certifies that what was not found does not exist.

reader_ladder.plain: Not finding evidence for something is not the same as showing it is false. You can only call an absence a 'no' inside a list you have fully checked; otherwise the honest answer is 'unknown'.

analogy.text: Not finding your keys in one drawer only tells you they are not in that drawer. It does not tell you they are gone.

maps.doctrine: the declared finite domain with complete coverage
maps.analogy: the one drawer you searched all the way through

maps.doctrine: the unknown verdict outside it
maps.analogy: the rest of the house you have not checked

analogy.boundary: The drawer picture works only when the search space is small and fully declared, like one drawer; it does not let you call something false across an open world you never finished searching.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops the system turning 'we did not see a problem' into 'there is no problem', which is how silent failures get signed off.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That no evidence means no problem. Outside a fully checked list, no evidence just means not yet known.

records.one_line: A partial computation returns a real answer only under its declared precondition; outside it, it returns a typed refusal carrying a reason and evidence, never a silent number or NaN.
records.deep: AX-7 refuses the most common way numerical code lies: producing a plausible-looking value where its preconditions do not actually hold. Microcosm requires a partial computation g to be totalized into a Result, so it returns Ok(y) only when a declared precondition Pre(x) is met, and otherwise returns a Refusal that names the reason and carries the evidence behind it. The refusal is the load-bearing half, because a number with no precondition check and no failure path is indistinguishable from a number that happens to be wrong; a NaN, an out-of-range horizon, or a missing dependency must surface as a typed object the caller can route, not leak downstream as a figure that reads as a result. This connects directly to the evidence model: a refusal with a reason is admissible information, whereas a silent number is a claim with no basis beneath it, so AX-7 is what lets the upstream admissibility floor see a gap instead of inheriting a fabricated value.

formal.reads: For every input, when the declared precondition holds the partial computation returns Ok carrying a value, and when it does not hold the computation returns a Refusal carrying a reason and its evidence.

symbols.meaning: a partial computation, totalized into a Result
the declared precondition on input x
a successful result carrying value y
a typed refusal carrying a reason r and evidence e

records.governs: What a partial computation is allowed to return when its precondition does not hold.
records.requires: A declared precondition for the Ok path and a typed Refusal carrying a reason and evidence on every other path, including missing dependencies and undefined values.
records.refuses: An inadmissible number or silent NaN returned where no precondition was checked.

example.text: The finance forecast evaluation spine returns a typed refusal naming the reason when a forecast horizon is at or above the sample size, or when the statistics dependency needed for a t-distribution is unavailable, instead of returning a number computed outside its domain.

counterexample.text: A statistics routine divides by a sample size that is too small and returns the resulting NaN as if it were a score, and a downstream page prints it as a real forecast number.

records.does_not_prove: Returning typed refusals at these named computations does not prove that every partial path in the system is totalized. It covers the declared preconditions and dependency gaps that are actually checked; uncovered partial functions remain a gap rather than a guaranteed refusal.

reader_ladder.plain: A calculation may return a real number only when the conditions it needs actually hold. When they don't, it must hand back a clearly labelled refusal that names the reason and the evidence, never a quiet number or a blank that the next step reads as an answer.

analogy.text: A cash machine that can't complete a withdrawal prints a stated decline reason and gives you nothing, rather than spitting out some arbitrary amount of cash that you'd carry off as if it were correct.

maps.doctrine: the precondition that must hold for a real result
maps.analogy: the conditions the machine needs to release cash, like a valid balance and a reachable account

maps.doctrine: Ok carrying a value when the precondition holds
maps.analogy: the machine dispensing the requested amount when everything checks out

maps.doctrine: a typed refusal carrying a reason and evidence
maps.analogy: the printed decline notice naming why it stopped

maps.doctrine: never a silent number or NaN leaking downstream
maps.analogy: never handing out an arbitrary stack of cash that you'd treat as a real withdrawal

analogy.boundary: The cash machine shows that a failed operation must decline with a reason instead of emitting a stray figure; it does not establish that every operation in the system declines this way, and untested failure paths can still leak a bad value rather than a clean refusal.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It means a missing input or an out-of-range condition shows up as a visible, routable gap instead of a plausible-looking figure that a later page prints as a genuine result.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a number coming out the end means the conditions were fine, so a calculation that quietly produced a value can be treated as a valid answer without checking it had grounds to run.

records.one_line: Provenance labels and ceilings travel with a value across every boundary; untrusted material reaches a privileged sink only through a declared transform.
records.deep: Trust is not a property of where a value ends up, it is a property of where it came from and what was done to it on the way. Every value carries a provenance class and a ceiling, and those travel with it across every boundary it crosses. Untrusted material, a prompt, a provider payload, or private-adjacent source, may reach a privileged or public sink only through a declared transform whose output the sink's policy admits. The endpoint cannot certify its own cleanliness: the label has to have been carried, not stamped on at the door. This is why a clean-looking final step is not enough, and why the system tracks how far a value may travel rather than just what it currently looks like.

formal.reads: For any flow from x into a sink s, either x's label already sits within the sink's policy, or s is the output of a declared transform T applied to x.

symbols.meaning: a value entering a flow
a sink (a public or privileged output)
x's provenance class and ceiling
what the sink is allowed to admit
a declared sanitising transform
the transform is declared, not stamped at the door

records.governs: How material and its trust label move across boundaries.
records.requires: A provenance class and ceiling carried with the value, and a declared transform before untrusted material reaches a privileged sink.
records.refuses: An endpoint asserting a clean label without that label having propagated.

example.text: The prompt-injection replay carries the information-flow policy with the data, so untrusted instructions arriving at a tool call are caught at the boundary instead of trusted because they reached a trusted endpoint.

counterexample.text: A public page prints a value lifted from a private-adjacent source because the final copy step looked clean, with no provenance carried across the copy.

records.does_not_prove: Catching labelled flows at named endpoints does not prove non-interference across the whole graph. General propagation is bounded by what is actually traced, and the registry records that debt rather than hiding it.

reader_ladder.plain: Where a value came from, and how far it is allowed to travel, must follow it everywhere. Untrusted material can only reach a sensitive place after passing through a stated cleaning step.

analogy.text: Allergen information has to follow an ingredient all the way through the kitchen. A clean-looking label on the final plate is not enough if nobody tracked the nuts.

maps.doctrine: the provenance label and ceiling carried with the value
maps.analogy: the allergen tag that travels with the ingredient

maps.doctrine: the declared transform before a sensitive output
maps.analogy: the documented step that removes or contains the allergen before serving

analogy.boundary: The kitchen-label picture shows that the tag must travel and be transformed on purpose; it does not capture the full tracing of how information flows and combines, which is stricter than one label.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It blocks private, provider, or unverified input quietly reaching a public or privileged output just because the final step looked clean.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a clean label at the end shows a clean path. Only a label that actually travelled, with a declared cleaning step, counts.

records.one_line: An effect may count as landed only with a compensator or a declared irreversible boundary; multi-step effects land as a saga under compare-and-swap and single-writer constraints.
records.deep: Microcosm treats a write as inadmissible until there is a way back from it or an explicit declaration that there is not. An effect counts as landed only if it carries a compensator that can reverse it or a declared irreversible boundary that marks the point of no return, and a multi-step effect lands as a saga whose every step compares against the head it expected and finds a single writer holding the path. The failure this refuses is the half-completed write: a sequence that succeeds partway, hits a moved head or a second writer, and leaves the world in a state nobody declared and nobody can undo. This ties back to the admissibility floor, because a landing that cannot be recomputed against its expected parent or rolled back is an effect asserting its own success with no check behind it, which is exactly the bare assertion the evidence order puts at the bottom.

formal.reads: An effect may land only if it declares a compensator or an irreversible boundary, and if it is multi-step, only when its expected parent still matches the head and at most one writer holds its path.

symbols.meaning: the effect e is treated as landed
a declared compensator that can reverse e
a declared irreversible boundary for e
the effect e is a multi-step effect
the compare-and-swap expected-parent check still holds
the single-writer constraint on e's path

records.governs: When a mutation is allowed to count as landed.
records.requires: A declared compensator or an irreversible boundary, and for multi-step effects a compare-and-swap expected-parent check plus a single-writer constraint on the path.
records.refuses: A blind irreversible write.

example.text: The mission transaction work spine lands a multi-step effect only when its expected parent still matches the head, so a run whose head advanced underneath it fails with a parent-mismatch rather than overwriting the newer state.

counterexample.text: A step writes to a shared path and is treated as landed because the call returned, with no expected-parent check and no single-writer constraint, so two concurrent writers interleave and the final state matches neither.

records.does_not_prove: Blocking parent-mismatch and same-path conflict on these named paths does not prove every effect in the system is compensable or every concurrent write is serialized. The legacy strong label is not a computed support verdict, and the routing registry still records that mapping these negative cases to a full rejection of the blind irreversible write remains unverified.

reader_ladder.plain: A change to the world only counts as done if there is a stated way to undo it, or a clear note saying it can never be undone. A multi-step change only goes through if the starting point it expected is still there and nobody else is changing the same thing at the same time.

analogy.text: A money transfer between accounts only completes if the bank can either reverse it or has marked it as final and non-refundable, and a multi-leg transfer only proceeds when the balance it started from has not changed and no second transfer is touching the same account.

maps.doctrine: an effect treated as landed
maps.analogy: a transfer treated as completed

maps.doctrine: a declared compensator that can reverse the effect
maps.analogy: the bank's ability to reverse the transfer

maps.doctrine: a declared irreversible boundary
maps.analogy: the transfer marked final and non-refundable

maps.doctrine: the compare-and-swap expected-parent check still holding
maps.analogy: the starting balance being unchanged when the leg runs

maps.doctrine: the single-writer constraint on the path
maps.analogy: no second transfer touching the same account at the same time

analogy.boundary: This only describes when a change is allowed to be called done; it does not show that every change in the system can be undone, and one transfer that passed its checks does not establish that all transfers are reversible or collision-free.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops a half-finished change from being silently treated as complete, which is how a system ends up in a state nobody chose and nobody can walk back.
reader_ladder.common_misread: Reading this as a promise that everything is reversible. It actually allows changes to be marked permanently final too; the rule is that the change must declare which one it is, not that undo is always possible.

records.one_line: A claim about live state is a cached read, valid only while it carries when it was taken, what it was read from, and how to recompute it.
records.deep: A number that describes a moving system stops being true the moment the system moves, so Microcosm treats every claim over live state as a cached read rather than a fact. To be admissible such a claim has to carry four things: the value, the time it was taken, the basis it was read from, and the rule for recomputing it; a bare value with none of these is a frozen snapshot wearing the costume of a current reading. This is where the evidence model meets time: the admissibility floor decides whether a claim counts at all, and this axiom adds that a live claim's strength decays with its own staleness unless its declared basis still holds. It bites hardest on the things that look most authoritative, the running counts and dashboard totals, because those are exactly the values a reader assumes are fresh.

formal.reads: A live-state claim is admissible only when it carries a value, an as-of time, a basis, and a rederive rule, and either its declared basis still holds at that time or the claim has been recomputed by that rule.

symbols.meaning: the claim varphi is admissible over live state
a claim over live state
the value asserted
the as-of time the value was taken
the basis the value was read from
the rule that recomputes the value from the basis
the declared basis is still valid as of t

records.governs: When a value read from live state is still allowed to be stated as current.
records.requires: A value carrying its as-of time, its basis, and a rederive rule, with invalidation or recomputation once that basis no longer holds.
records.refuses: A frozen live count stated without a refresh basis.

example.text: The fact-claim audit flags a volatile numeric claim that carries no freshness binding as a stable blocking code, so a live count cannot be published as if it were still current without declaring when it was taken and how to recompute it.

counterexample.text: A page prints a running total once and leaves it on display for weeks, with no as-of time and no rule to recompute it, so a stale snapshot reads as a live figure.

records.does_not_prove: Blocking unbound volatile numbers does not make any specific number current or correct, and it does not establish that staleness is caught everywhere. The freshness component of this axiom's own ceiling stays an ordered unknown until a source-owned refresh contract exists, so this object names the obligation rather than certifying that it is met.

reader_ladder.plain: A number about something that keeps changing is just a reading taken at a moment, not a permanent fact. To be usable later it has to say when it was read, where it was read from, and how to read it again.

analogy.text: An electricity meter reading is only useful on a bill if it records the date it was taken, which meter it came from, and how to take a fresh reading; the bare number alone tells you nothing about today's usage.

maps.analogy: a number describing ongoing electricity usage

maps.analogy: the figure shown on the meter

maps.doctrine: the as-of time the value was taken
maps.analogy: the date the reading was recorded

maps.doctrine: the basis the value was read from
maps.analogy: which meter the reading came from

maps.doctrine: the rule that recomputes the value
maps.analogy: the procedure for taking a fresh reading

analogy.boundary: This only covers when a live number can still be stated as current; it does not establish that any particular reading is right now, and a reading that carries its date is not therefore fresh today.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It keeps a number that was true once from quietly being read as if it were still true weeks later, after the thing it measured has moved on.
reader_ladder.common_misread: Thinking that attaching a date and a source makes the number current. It only makes the number checkable; whether it still holds has to be read again from the source.

records.one_line: A doctrine artifact may claim authority only when an executable grammar accepts its shape and it carries receipt obligations and an anti-claim; prose alone is a projection.
records.deep: AX-11 turns the doctrine itself into something the system can execute rather than merely read. A standard, contract, or rule may carry authority only when an executable grammar accepts its form and it arrives with its receipt obligations and its own anti-claim attached; a clause that is only well written sits at the level of a projection, not a law. This is what stops a confident standard from governing by rhetoric: the gate decides on the artifact's checkable shape, not its prose, so the same admissibility discipline AX-1 imposes on claims is imposed on the documents that define how claims are judged. The bite is the second negative case as much as the first: passing the grammar admits the artifact's shape and never certifies that the doctrine it expresses is complete, so a grammar pass that is read as coverage is rejected, not rewarded.

formal.reads: A doctrine artifact carries authority only when an executable grammar accepts it and it presents receipt obligations and an anti-claim; otherwise it is held as a projection.

symbols.meaning: the doctrine artifact d carries governing authority
a doctrine artifact (standard, contract, rule)
the language an executable grammar G accepts

the projection tier, below authority

records.governs: Whether a doctrine artifact may govern, or is only a readable projection of one.
records.requires: Acceptance by an executable grammar plus carried receipt obligations and a declared anti-claim, not well-formed prose.
records.refuses: Prose claiming executable authority.

example.text: The executable doctrine grammar component accepts a standard only when its shape parses and its receipt obligations and anti-claim are present, and rejects a prose standard that asserts authority without that executable form.

counterexample.text: A persuasive standard written in prose is treated as binding because it reads authoritatively, with no grammar accepting its shape and no anti-claim or receipt obligations carried.

records.does_not_prove: A grammar accepting an artifact's shape does not prove the doctrine it expresses is complete or correct; admitting the form is not certifying the coverage, and this reader page is itself a projection that cannot raise the authority of the source it explains.

reader_ladder.plain: A rule is only allowed to govern when it passes a fixed format check and comes with the evidence it must produce and a clear statement of what it does not cover. A well-written paragraph on its own is just something to read, not a rule.

analogy.text: A customs declaration only clears goods when it passes the required form check, arrives with its supporting paperwork, and states plainly what it does not vouch for; a politely worded letter describing the shipment, however convincing, clears nothing.

maps.analogy: a declaration that clears the goods

maps.analogy: the supporting paperwork that must travel with it

maps.doctrine: the declared statement of what it does not establish
maps.analogy: the declaration's stated exclusions, what it does not vouch for

maps.doctrine: prose held only as a readable projection
maps.analogy: the convincing letter that clears nothing

analogy.boundary: This only governs whether a rule is allowed to bind at all; passing the format check does not show the rule is complete or correct, and a cleared declaration is not therefore an accurate account of the goods.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops a confident-sounding paragraph from being treated as an enforced rule just because it reads authoritatively, keeping unchecked prose out of the seat of governance.
reader_ladder.common_misread: Assuming that passing the format check means the rule fully covers its subject. Passing the check admits the rule's shape only; it never certifies that what the rule says is complete.

records.one_line: Every claim Microcosm makes about itself passes the same admissibility floor it imposes on everything else; the meta-layer gets no exemption.
records.deep: A system that exempts its own claims from its own floor is just asserting with extra steps. Microcosm removes the exemption: every claim it makes about itself, in a generated page, a validator, a result record, or a coverage projection, has to pass the same admissibility relation it imposes on any external claim. The support evaluator is itself subject to the support floor; this very page is a projection held below the source it describes and cannot raise that source's authority. Removing the escape hatch is what lets the system state its own limits without that statement quietly becoming a new unchecked claim.

formal.reads: For every Microcosm artifact and every claim it carries, the claim is admitted under the same relation as an external claim.

symbols.meaning: the Microcosm artifacts, including its own pages and validators
any such artifact
a claim the artifact carries
the claims the artifact carries
the admissibility floor from AX-1
the same admissibility relation applied to an external claim

records.governs: Whether the system's own outputs get a discount.
records.requires: That meta artifacts, pages, validators, records, and projections, meet the same evidence and refusal floor as everything else.
records.refuses: A meta-artifact exemption.

example.text: The release wording gate runs the same claim-language floor over Microcosm's own public copy that it imposes on any other shard, blocking overclaiming language in the system's own pages.

counterexample.text: A system publishes a self-assessment of "verified and complete" that would never pass the evidence floor it applies to the work it audits.

records.does_not_prove: Applying the floor to its own artifacts does not make the system complete or correct. It only removes the special pleading; the limits remain, stated rather than hidden.

reader_ladder.plain: The system holds its own statements about itself to the same standard it holds everyone else's. It gets no discount for being the one doing the checking.

analogy.text: A referee cannot switch to an easier rulebook when the call is about their own team. The same rules apply to them.

maps.doctrine: Microcosm's own claims about itself
maps.analogy: the referee judging their own team

maps.doctrine: the same admissibility floor as external claims
maps.analogy: the single rulebook that applies to everyone

analogy.boundary: The referee picture shows no special pleading; it does not say the system is therefore complete or correct, only that it cannot exempt itself.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It lets the system state its own limits without that statement quietly becoming a new unchecked claim.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That applying the rule to itself makes the system trustworthy by default. It only removes the exemption; the limits remain, stated rather than hidden.

records.one_line: Recompute a verdict from lower-level evidence instead of repeating a label, and keep a negative fixture where the cheap lie would slip through.
records.deep: P-1 is AX-1 turned into a habit a builder repeats before anything is allowed to mean something. Do not trust a fixture's own verdict, a route status, a count, or a public copy line as proof; recompute it from the evidence underneath and keep a negative fixture that fails when the cheap lie is tried. The negative fixture is the load-bearing half: a check that only ever sees the happy path proves nothing, because it never had the chance to reject. This is the principle that makes "green" mean recomputed rather than reported.

formal.reads: Trust a verdict only if a checker recomputes it from the basis and also rejects the negative fixture built to fool it.

symbols.meaning: a builder is willing to trust the verdict on a claim
the verdict being claimed
the verdict a checker recomputes from the basis
the lower-level evidence the checker runs over
a negative fixture built so the cheap lie should fail
the checker's refusal on that negative fixture

records.governs: The claim boundary, before a result is allowed to mean anything.
records.requires: Recomputation from lower-level evidence plus a negative fixture that the checker rejects.
records.refuses: Echoing a declared verdict, label, or count as if repetition made it true.

example.text: The benchmark-integrity replay recomputes a score from the run rather than trusting the reported number, and keeps gaming attempts as negative cases that must be caught.

counterexample.text: A summary repeats a component's self-reported "passed" into a status board without ever recomputing it or keeping a case that would fail.

records.does_not_prove: Recomputing one verdict from evidence does not make the principle a witness for AX-1; it operationalises specific obligations and inherits no authority beyond them.

reader_ladder.plain: Trust a result only after re-deriving it from the underlying evidence, and keep a deliberately wrong test case that the check is required to reject.

analogy.text: A teacher re-works the exam answer from scratch instead of copying the answer key, and also keeps a wrong-answer version to confirm the marking actually catches mistakes.

maps.doctrine: recomputing the verdict from the evidence
maps.analogy: re-working the answer instead of copying the key

maps.doctrine: the negative case the checker must reject
maps.analogy: the planted wrong answer the marking must catch

analogy.boundary: The re-marking picture shows recomputation plus a trap for false passes; it does not capture every kind of evidence a real check runs over.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: Without it, a label that says 'passed' gets repeated down the line and nobody ever re-derives whether it was true.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That re-checking once makes a result permanently safe. It only earns trust for that result, against that check.

records.one_line: A claim may be no stronger than what its named checker actually decides; a checker that settles a local contract cannot license prose that projects global authority.
records.deep: P-2 is the strength half of the admissibility floor: AX-1 caps a claim at the strength of the check that accepts it, and AX-5 makes that ceiling the meet of the parts, so the strongest honest claim equals the weakest component the checker actually settles. Its bite is distinct from "recompute, do not echo": even a genuinely recomputed verdict overclaims if the prose around it reaches past what the checker decided, for example a validator that confirms a record's shape being read as proof of the property the record names. The cover calculus enforces this by computing each claim ceiling from the specific grounding obligations, never from whole-axiom labels and never by hand-stamping; support for the principle is bounded by the covers of the obligations beneath it. This is the rule that keeps a local-contract checker, a registry, or a single witness route from being narrated into a system-wide guarantee.

formal.reads: A claim's strength is bounded above by the meet of the strengths of exactly the checks that actually decide it; the ceiling is the weakest deciding component, not the most impressive one.

symbols.meaning: the strength a claim is allowed to assert
the claim being made
a checker, validator, registry, or witness route that decides part of the claim
the basis the deciding checks run over
meet: the weakest of the deciding components
the set of checks that actually settle the claim, excluding ones it merely names

records.governs: The strength boundary, where a claim is sized to the check that accepts it.
records.requires: Capping a claim at the meet of what its named checker, validator, registry, or witness route actually computes, with the ceiling derived from the grounding obligations rather than asserted.
records.refuses: Letting a checker that decides only a local contract license prose that projects global or system-wide authority.

example.text: The formal-math readiness gate computes a readiness boundary and holds its claim to that boundary, reporting how far the available evidence reaches rather than narrating a local check into a proof of readiness.

counterexample.text: A component validates that a result record has the expected fields, and a summary page reads that shape check as evidence the underlying property holds across the whole system.

records.does_not_prove: Holding a claim to its checker's strength does not raise that checker, and the principle is not a witness for AX-1 or AX-5; it operationalises specific grounding obligations and inherits no support beyond the covers computed for them. The worked example illustrates the rule for a reader and never counts as support that the principle is obligation-backed or complete.

reader_ladder.plain: A statement can only be as strong as the exact thing the check actually settled. If a check confirms one small, local fact, the writing around it must not stretch that into a sweeping claim about the whole system.

analogy.text: A blood test that measures one thing, like iron levels, tells you about iron. It does not let you write 'this person is in perfect health' on the chart.

maps.doctrine: the claim's strength is capped at what the named check decides
maps.analogy: the iron test only supports a statement about iron

maps.doctrine: prose overreaching past the check into system-wide authority
maps.analogy: writing 'perfectly healthy' off one narrow blood test

analogy.boundary: The blood-test picture shows that a result is limited to what was measured; it does not show how several separate checks are combined, where the overall ceiling drops to the weakest one. It is an illustration, not support that the rule holds everywhere.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops a narrow, genuine check from being written up as a broad system-wide claim, so readers can size a claim to what was really tested.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That because the underlying check was real, any confident sentence built on it is also safe. The check sets a ceiling; words above that ceiling are not earned and do not prove the wider claim.

records.one_line: Put trust in a small checker that rereads a certificate and decides, and keep the large producer outside that authority boundary.
records.deep: P-3 is AX-2 turned into a building habit: the thing you trust should be small enough to read and rerun, while the thing that did the work may be as large and opaque as it likes. A big model, search, or pipeline emits a certificate; a small verifier, parser, harness, compiler route, or registry contract rereads that certificate and decides, and only the checker sits inside the trusted boundary. This connects directly to the claim and evidence model: a claim's strength is capped at the strength of the check that admits it, so shrinking the checker shrinks what you have to believe to believe the verdict. The load-bearing move is keeping the producer outside the boundary; once the thing being judged also gets to decide, the check has collapsed into the same narrative confidence it was meant to replace.

formal.reads: Trust a verdict only when a small checker rereads a certificate and accepts, the trusted base is that checker alone, and the producer that emitted the certificate is not inside it.

symbols.meaning: a builder is willing to trust the verdict on a claim
the claim being trusted
the small checker that decides
the certificate the producer emits
the trusted base the claim rests on
the large component that emitted the certificate, kept outside the trusted base

records.governs: Where trust is concentrated, between the large producer and the small thing that decides.
records.requires: A small checker that rereads a certificate and accepts, with the producer kept outside the trusted boundary.
records.refuses: Trusting a large producer's own confidence in place of a small, inspectable, rerunnable check.

example.text: The certificate kernel lab keeps producers separate from the checker: a producer emits certificate material and a small kernel rereads and decides, so the authority lives in the part you can inspect and rerun rather than in the producer.

counterexample.text: A large model is trusted because it sounds sure of its answer, with no small separate checker rereading anything, so the producer is also the judge.

records.does_not_prove: Concentrating trust in a small checker does not make the principle a witness for AX-2, and a small checker is not automatically a correct or complete one. It operationalises specific obligations and inherits no authority beyond them; admission, support, and this reader illustration stay independent.

reader_ladder.plain: Put your trust in one small, simple step that re-does the work and decides, while letting the big complicated thing that produced the answer stay outside that decision. The thing being judged should not get to be its own judge.

analogy.text: At a self-checkout, the small weighing plate re-weighs each item to confirm what was scanned. The shopper, who knows what is in the bag, is never the one who decides the bag is correct.

maps.doctrine: the small checker that rereads and decides
maps.analogy: the weighing plate that re-checks each item

maps.doctrine: the large producer kept outside the trusted decision
maps.analogy: the shopper, who is not allowed to confirm their own bag

analogy.boundary: The weighing-plate picture shows where the deciding power sits and that the producer is kept out; it does not show that a small checker is automatically a correct or complete one. A simple checker can still be wrong, and this is only an illustration.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: Keeping the deciding part small means the part you actually have to audit and re-run stays small, even as the systems producing answers grow large and hard to inspect.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a confident, capable producer can be trusted to vouch for itself. Once the thing being judged also decides, there is no real check left, only restated confidence.

records.one_line: Authority to mutate is derived from dereferenced proof, policy, and current world state; a credential, role name, or trusted-session phrasing authorizes nothing on its own.
records.deep: P-4 is the authority leg of the claim model, the way P-1 is the evidence leg: before an effect lands, recompute whether it is authorized rather than reading authority off who is asking. Authorization for a subject and an effect comes from dereferenced proof references, the governing policy, rollback or result-record evidence, and current world state; standing credentials, an admin role name, or trusted-session phrasing are ambient identity, which carries no authority by itself. This is the confused-deputy failure stated as a rule: a component holding a powerful capability must still derive permission for each effect, and tool authority is bounded by declared identity, scope, approval, and side-effect records, not by the tool merely being available. It bites because possession reads as permission almost everywhere, so a pipeline that trusts a present credential passes its own path while granting reach nothing actually authorized.

formal.reads: A subject is authorized for an effect exactly when the authorizer's derivation over the dereferenced tokens, governing policy, proof references, and world state grants it; holding an ambient credential or role name never on its own implies a grant.

symbols.meaning: a subject is authorized for an effect
a subject requesting an effect
the effect or mutation it wants to land

records.governs: The authority boundary, before any effect or mutation is allowed to land.
records.requires: Deriving authorization from dereferenced proof, policy, and world state, with tool effects bounded by declared identity, scope, approval, and side-effect records.
records.refuses: Treating a standing credential, role name, admin phrasing, trusted session, or tool availability as authority on its own.

example.text: The tool-authority replay binds each tool effect to a declared scope and approval and rejects an overbroad scope or an unapproved side effect, so reach is derived from declared authority rather than from the tool being callable.

counterexample.text: A mutation is allowed because the caller holds a valid admin credential, with nothing dereferencing that credential to the specific effect or checking it against policy and current state.

records.does_not_prove: Deriving authorization in these components does not make P-4 a witness for AX-3 or raise that axiom's support ceiling; it operationalises the specific obligations that authorization is derived, that standing credentials are insufficient, and that tool effects require declared scope, and inherits no authority beyond them. The worked example is reader illustration and computes no support.

reader_ladder.plain: Holding a credential, a role name, or being inside a 'trusted' session does not by itself give permission to make a change. Permission is worked out fresh for each specific action, from the rules and the current situation.

analogy.text: Knowing someone's bank card number does not let money move. For each payment, the bank checks this card, this amount, and the live account state before it lets the transfer go through.

maps.doctrine: the standing credential or role name that is not authority on its own
maps.analogy: merely having the card number

maps.doctrine: authority derived per action from policy and current world state
maps.analogy: the bank deciding each payment against this account, right now

analogy.boundary: The bank-transfer picture shows that holding a credential is not the same as being cleared to act; it does not capture everything weighed at decision time, such as the specific permissions, the rules, and the side-effect records. It is an illustration only.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It closes the gap where a component that holds broad standing power is talked into using it for someone else, since each action still has to earn its own permission.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a valid credential is enough to act. The credential is one input; the actual change still has to be cleared for that exact action against the current state.

records.one_line: A reused result is valid only if it carries the digest of the exact content it was computed from; drift in that content forces a recompute, and a name match alone licenses nothing.
records.deep: P-5 turns content-addressed determinism into a habit a builder repeats before reusing any prior work. A stored result record, a coalesced command run, an imported source copy, or a resumed work-landing attempt may be reused only if it carries the digest of the exact content it was derived from; equal content basis permits reuse, and drift in the source bytes, the dirty scoped files, the parent commit, or the fixture input forces a recompute or a refusal. The bite is that a name, a path, or a label is a cheap stand-in for a basis: cache by name and you serve a stale answer the moment the content moves underneath the name. This connects straight to the claim and evidence model, because a reused verdict inherits its scope limit from the basis it was actually checked over, and reuse across drift silently widens that scope to content nothing ever verified.

formal.reads: A prior result may be reused for an incoming basis only when the result's stored content id equals the digest of that basis and the basis is non-empty; otherwise recompute.

a prior result eligible for reuse
the content basis of the incoming request
the digest over that content basis

a missing basis, which is bottom and licenses no reuse

records.governs: The reuse boundary, where a prior result is about to be served instead of recomputed.
records.requires: A declared content basis carried as a digest with the result, equal-basis reuse only, and a recompute or refusal the moment the source digest, scoped files, parent commit, or fixture input drifts.
records.refuses: Serving a cached result keyed on a name, path, or label after the content behind it has drifted.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings non-secret material in by manifest and digest, so when the source bytes drift the imported copy is recomputed rather than reused under the same name.

counterexample.text: A resumed work-landing attempt reuses an earlier result because the task name matches, even though the parent commit and scoped files have changed underneath it.

records.does_not_prove: Carrying a content basis on the reuse paths shown here does not prove reuse is sound everywhere; one freshness-basis mismatch remains a declared open gap, and this card and its example are reader illustration that compute no support for the principle or its grounding axiom.

reader_ladder.plain: A saved result can be reused only if it is tagged with the exact inputs it was made from, and those inputs still match. If the inputs have changed, you must redo the work; a matching name or label alone is never enough.

analogy.text: A warranty repair is only valid for the exact unit whose serial number is on the claim. If a part with a different serial was swapped in, a matching model name on the box does not make the old warranty apply.

maps.doctrine: reuse only when the stored content tag matches the current inputs
maps.analogy: the warranty holds only when the serial number still matches the unit

maps.doctrine: a name or label match alone licensing reuse
maps.analogy: trusting the model name on the box instead of the serial

analogy.boundary: The serial-number picture shows that identity must match the exact item, not just the label; it does not cover every kind of input drift the rule watches, such as changed source bytes, edited files, or a moved parent. It only illustrates the rule.

reader_ladder.why_it_matters: It stops a stale or tampered result being served as fresh just because its name or path still looks familiar after the content underneath has moved.
reader_ladder.common_misread: That a matching name or path means the cached answer is still good. Only a match on the actual content the result was computed from licenses reuse; otherwise the work has to be redone.

records.one_line: A composite is only as trusted as its weakest part; missing evidence defaults to blocked, and a later stage can lower status but never raise it without a new derivation.
records.deep: P-6 is AX-5 turned into the default a builder relies on when evidence is absent rather than negative. The status of a composite is the meet of its parts, so a single missing policy file, source digest, secret scan, negative case, or result self-scan pulls the whole verdict down to blocked instead of leaving a gap that silently reads as pass. The bite is in the asymmetry: a later stage may lower a status freely, but it cannot raise one without a fresh derivation, which is exactly why a downstream view can explain why something passed yet can never upgrade a source truth that was blocked underneath it. This is the principle that makes silence cost something, and it is what lets the claim and evidence model treat an unscanned or unproven input as the bottom of the order rather than a weak yes.

formal.reads: A composite's status is the meet of its parts' statuses; a part with no evidence sits at blocked; and a later status may exceed an earlier one only when a new derivation justifies the rise.

symbols.meaning: the status assigned to a composite or part, ordered with blocked at the bottom
a composite whose status is being computed
meet: the weakest of the part statuses
a part the composite is built from
the parts the composite is built from

no admissible evidence for that part
